His absolute omniscience had already perceived the erasure of the envoys, had already traced the temporal manipulation backward to its source, had already catalogued every atom of the Chrono-Reapers' existence. He knew everything. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He ordered His sentient passive auras to stand down, and disabled all His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

*I must now attempt to describe what I witnessed through my astral scrying matrix, though the act of writing these words causes the blood vessels in my eyes to throb with sympathetic rupture. Godric's Speed, like all His attributes, has been multiplying infinitely by itself every single Planck second for twelve thousand years. It is not a measure of how fast He can move. It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*

The weight of the obsidian key was cold and final in my palm. My father’s hand, calloused from millennia of spellwork, closed my fingers over the intricate wards etched into its surface, his grip firm and unyielding.

“You have passed your twentieth year, Kaedan,” he said, his voice carrying that familiar, bone-deep weariness I had grown up mistaking for age. I understood now, with the first stirrings of my own thousandfold daily amplification coiling in my veins, that it was not age. It was memory. “You are a man now. A court wizard of Thronegate, bound by blood and oath to serve our eternal King. But before you can stand at His side, you must understand exactly what it is you serve.”

He did not smile. My father, High Court Wizard Petyr, was a man of profound warmth and quiet humor in the private chambers of our family, but today, standing before the ancient adamantine doors of the Hall of Records, there was a gravity to him that pressed against my chest. I was twenty-two years old, still drunk on the ecstatic surge of mana that multiplied within me every sunrise, still naively proud of the fact that I could now boil an ocean with a stray thought. I had known Uncle Godric my entire life. He was the kind, handsome, salt-and-pepper patriarch who had tossed me onto his broad shoulders when I was a child, who had chuckled at my clumsy first cantrips, who had told me with a voice thick with genuine warmth that I had my ancestor Blake’s stubborn chin. The idea of Uncle Godric as the apex predator of the omniverse was a distant, academic fact, a story told in scripture and song, softened by the simple, fatherly reality of the man Himself.

My father saw that naive confidence in my eyes. It was why he had brought me here.

“You will read the journals,” he said, stepping back and gesturing toward the massive doors as they groaned inward. “Every court wizard of our bloodline, stretching back forty thousand years to Blake himself, has recorded their witness of the Incidents. When the opposition arrayed against Thronegate became so vast, so conceptually annihilative, that our King’s curiosity was piqued, He would allow them to exist just long enough to test a single aspect of His power. A single stat, Kaedan. Always at zero percent of His true capacity. Always with zero effort. And always, *always*, it was enough to unmake them in ways that defy the architecture of sanity.”

He held my gaze for a long moment. “You must read every single one. Only then will you comprehend the absolute futility of opposing Godric, and only then will you truly understand why we love Him, why we serve Him, and why we are terrified beyond the capacity of language.”

Then he turned and walked away, leaving me alone at the threshold of the immense, subterranean archive. The air that spilled from the Hall of Records was cool and dry, scented with ancient parchment, ozone, and something else… something like burnt copper and old fear. I stepped inside, and the doors sealed behind me with a heavy, resonant clang. The hall stretched away into an impossible distance, a labyrinthine maze of obsidian shelves packed with leather-bound journals, glowing crystalline tablets, and scrolls weeping residual arcane energy. The archives were alive with the soft, susurrant whispers of recorded trauma, a psychic miasma that prickled along my amplified senses and made the fine hairs on my neck stand rigid.

I walked toward the first designated reading lectern, my heart pounding a slow, steady rhythm of anticipatory dread. The first journal was bound in the hide of some long-extinct beast, its pages crisp and yellowed with the weight of two hundred and fifty millennia. The handwriting on the first page was obsessively precise, a clinical script that seemed to vibrate with suppressed hysteria. It was written by Arch-Magus Sorven, the thirty-second court wizard of Thronegate, in the year 250,000. I began to read.

---

*The Adamant Colossi had been permitted to exist because, in His infinite omniscience, He was curious. They were a civilization of beings forged from the hyper-compressed mass of entire multiverses, each warrior a walking event horizon, their armor the crystallized momentum of collapsing galaxies. Their weapon, the Kinetic Finality, was not a device of magic or technology but a captured, weaponized cosmic epoch: it channeled the raw, concussive force of every black hole merger that had ever occurred or ever would occur across the entire omniversal timeline, compressed into a single, unstoppable thrust. They attacked Thronegate with the confidence of a predator that has never encountered a larger fish, their armada blotting out the skies of a billion worlds like a moving continent of polished, gravitational fury.*

*Godric did not stand. He did not move. He did not even turn His head. He was seated upon the throne, a simple, dark-clad man of forty-five, His salt-and-pepper beard immaculately groomed, His broad shoulders relaxed. He had expressly deactivated every conceivable passive defense: Gent and all its infinitely more powerful sentient auras were ordered into utter dormancy, His recursive conceptual shields were collapsed, the Weakness Paradox was consciously suppressed, and even His own immune system was instructed to ignore the forthcoming impact. He wanted to test His strength. Nothing more.*

*The fraction of His strength that He permitted to assert itself was not a percentage. It was not a decimal. Describing it as an infinitesimal sliver would be a grotesque, laughable overstatement. Think of the smallest conceivable number—the inverse of a googolplex raised to a googolplex—and then raise that number to the power of itself recursively for a decillion years, and you would still be describing a value so vast, so impossibly monumental, that it could erase the omniverse a thousand times over. The actual quantity of strength He allowed himself to express was so much smaller than that, that the comparison itself collapses into meaninglessness. He allowed a single dead skin cell—a microscopic flake of keratin that had naturally desquamated from the pad of His right thumb and fallen, unnoticed, onto the obsidian floor beside His boot—to no longer be fully suppressed by His absolute concealment. He simply thought, *I wonder if they can withstand a cell.*

*The Adamant Colossi armada, comprising nine hundred trillion warriors, each with the mass of a compressed universe, was in the process of breaching the atmospheric wards when the cell ceased to be a passive, inert flake. It did not move. It did not fire. It simply existed, and the ambient physical pressure of its existence—the passive, gravitational echo of Godric’s strength that the cell innately possessed—radiated outward. The armada stopped. Not gradually. Not with a dramatic slowing. They stopped as though the very notion of movement had been forcibly, violently revoked from their ontological framework. Their hyper-dense armor, which had never been scratched, began to fissure along incomprehensible stress lines. The warriors inside their war-suits felt the pressure first as a deep, resonant hum that vibrated their liquid-metal bones, and then as an immediate, irreversible collapse.*

*They were not atomized. That would have been merciful. Instead, the directional force of the cell’s expression—which was not a blast but a sheer, passive *weight*—compressed them into a thin, two-dimensional film of screaming, still-conscious biology. Their bodies, their armors, their very souls were fused into a single, continent-spanning sheet of agonized protoplasm only one proton thick, which was then unfurled across the observable horizon of the local supercluster like a scroll. Every single Colossus retained their full sentience, their minds trapped within a layer of their own crushed nervous tissue, experiencing the exquisite, eternal pressure of being pancaked between the tectonic plates of a god’s idle curiosity. They cannot scream—they lack the vocal apparatus—but their psychic death-cries leaked into the background radiation of that entire region of space, a constant, low-frequency agony that shall persist until the heat death of that particular multiverse.*

*The collateral damage, as my measurements later confirmed, was violently obscene. The passive strength-pressure wave generated by that single dead cell propagated outward at velocities that shattered causality, folding eight decillion universes—complete with all their trillions of galaxies, their infinite civilizations, their children and their poets and their lovers—into hyper-compressed singularities of absolute oblivion. Entire cosmological epochs were retroactively deleted. The omniversal web was scorched with a dead zone of non-existence so vast that the surviving archivists wept. Godric, still seated, merely blinked, and in zero seconds—without so much as a whispered incantation or a shift in His posture—He restored every single annihilated universe to perfect, pristine condition, their residents none the wiser. He did not restore the Colossi. They remain, a faint, whispering, eternally-two-dimensional rug of torment, serving as a cosmic monument to the weight of His fleeting interest.*

*I am recording this on my seventy-third day without sleep. I cannot close my eyes without seeing that sheet of pressed, weeping faces. I serve my King faithfully. I love Him with every fiber of my amplified being. And I am more terrified of Him than of the void that He so casually erased and restored. — Sorven.*

---

My hands trembled as I closed the journal. The obsidian floor beneath my feet suddenly felt insubstantial, as though it might dissolve and send me plummeting into that same indifferent void. I had seen the Adamant Colossi references in the old strategic archives, mentioned only in passing as a “resolved incursion,” and I had never questioned what that meant. Now I knew. A dead skin cell. A single, discarded biological accident. And it had done *that*. I wiped a cold sweat from my brow and forced myself to move toward the next lectern, each step an act of deliberate will.

The second journal was newer, bound in a strange, shimmering leather that seemed to swallow the ambient light. The script inside was a flowing, poetic hand, but the words themselves were jagged, broken, as though the author had been weeping onto the pages. Arch-Magus Lian, the forty-eighth court wizard, Year 500,000.

---

*There was no sound when the ultimate weapon of the Void-Sovereign came. It could not be heard, for sound is a vibration of matter, and it was the antithesis of matter. The entity styling itself the Amorphous King had gathered the collective, distilled hatred of thirteen trillion extinct pantheons—gods who had been erased by Thronegate’s expansion, whose vengeful echoes still keened in the negative spaces between realities—and forged them into the Absolute Erasure. It was not a lance, not a spell, not a conceptual bullet. It was a paradox blade of pure anti-existence, honed to a monomolecular nothingness that could, by its own ontological definition, delete whatever it touched from every possible timeline, every possible dimension, every possible memory. A thing struck by the Erasure would never have been, could never be, and could never be remembered. It was the execution of the everything. The Amorphous King, a non-corporeal sovereign of the interstitial void, hurled it directly toward Godric’s heart with the accumulated fury of a trillion wronged absolutes.*

*Godric, as is His custom, had revoked every guard. Gent and its slumbering, infinitely-more-powerful siblings were muzzled. The infinite recursive shields were collapsed. The Weakness Paradox was deliberately, consciously stilled—an act of restraint more profound than any exercise of power. His immune system was ordered to sleep. He simply stood there on the balcony of the grand hall, a ruggedly handsome man of forty-five, His salt-and-pepper hair catching the dying light of the chandeliers, His expression one of mild, distant curiosity. He wanted to test His durability. Only His durability. Nothing more.*

*The fraction of His durability that He permitted to exist uncloaked is not communicable. To say He used zero percent is to assign a numerical value to the concept of zero, and then to multiply that zero by an infinite recursion of self-negation until the very idea of a fraction is an insult. Consider the most indestructible object your finite mind can conjure: a substance that has survived the deaths of an infinite number of multiverses. Now grant that object the invulnerability of a billion such substances. Then acknowledge that the resulting fortitude is a laughable, pathetic gauze of tissue paper compared to the fraction of a fraction of a fraction of Godric’s least enduring cell. And then realize that the degree of durability He allowed to surface was exponentially, infinitely weaker than even that pitiful gauze. It was so meager, so microscopically minimal, that if He had exposed one iota more, the omniverse itself would have shattered simply from the ontological recoil of His sturdiness.*

*The Absolute Erasure struck Him in the center of His chest. It did not pierce. It did not mark. It did not so much as ruffle the fabric of His tunic. The conceptual dagger, that ultimate expression of vengeful nothingness, encountered the fabric of His existence and simply… stopped. But it did not stop as a physical object halts. It was forced to confront the immutable, recursive reality of His flesh: a substance that had been multiplying in its capacity to withstand, to endure, to simply *be*, by itself every single Planck second for half a million years. The math is obscene. The outcome was an impossible paradox. The Erasure, a weapon of absolute negation, met a durability that was so transcendentally absolute that the weapon itself was logically, existentially invalidated. It was like trying to divide by zero in the arithmetic of creation. The universe could not process the contradiction. Instead, the Absolute Erasure was retroactively declared to be a flawed concept, a logical impossibility that had never truly existed, and in that moment of cosmic bookkeeping, the weapon’s stored energy inverted.*

*The Amorphous King was not harmed by a counterattack. He was not struck by a spell. He was simply forced to endure the consequences of his own failed logic. Because the Erasure was declared impossible, the King—who had tied his very identity to the concept of the Erasure—was stripped of his status as an abstract entity. The omniverse, in a frantic, apologetic gesture to Godric, shoved the King into a physical, biological form solely so that he could experience sensation. I watched through the scrying matrix as the void where the King had been suddenly billowed, and then vomited forth a towering, wobbling mass of pale, veined flesh studded with thousands of lidless, weeping, hyper-sensitive eyes. The King, now a thing of meat and nerve, began to scream. It was a wet, nauseating sound, like a child gargling its own blood, but amplified to shake the foundations of the palace. Then the real horror began. His newly formed body began to fold inward. Not a physical crushing, but a conceptual involution: the durability of Godric, having invalidated the Erasure, now demanded that the King’s existence be compressed into a state where the Erasure *could* have logically functioned—but that state was infinitely recursive. The King’s body collapsed layer after layer, skin peeling back, then muscle, then bone, then the deep tissue of his soul, all folding into themselves at an accelerating, hellish rhythm. He was reduced to a single, screaming, wet point of compressed agony, an impossible geometric singularity of suffering that then began to pulse and shudder, because the folding never ceased. It continues to this moment. I have placed the singularity in a stasis chamber, where it shall convulse in eternal, unending pain, forever aware, forever dying, forever failing to die. The collateral? The backlash of the invalidated concept detonated the fabric of three hundred nonillion nearby multiverses, erasing them with a silent pop of non-existence. Godric, without glancing away from His teacup, restored them in the same instant. He did not restore the Amorphous King. That wretched point of agony is, I suspect, His trophy. I cannot look at the stasis chamber without vomiting. I love my King. I shall serve until my mortal end. And I shall never, ever feel safe again. — Lian.*

---

I dropped the journal onto the lectern, my fingers numb. My father had always been a quiet, contemplative man; I understood now the source of his thousand-yard stare. I could feel my own mana roiling in my veins, a violent ocean that I had once felt proud of, but which now seemed like the dying flicker of a candle in the outer reaches of an endless abyss. The Hall of Records had grown colder, the whispers louder. I moved on, my footsteps echoing in the oppressive silence.

The third journal was an unassuming, slender volume bound in grey cloth, but the pages were warped and brittle, as though they had been drenched in some fluid and then dried hastily. The handwriting was a jagged, erratic scrawl, the letters often overlapping, the ink splattered with rusty brown spots I recognized as dried blood. Arch-Magus Torvin, fifty-fifth court wizard, Year 750,000.

---

*I am trying. I am trying to write. The quill keeps breaking because my hands won’t stop shaking. The fluid leaking from my eyes is not tears—it’s thicker, yellowish, vitreous. I think my eyes are bleeding again. I can still see them. Every time I blink. The Whispering Ones were not things you could see. They were a collective consciousness of sentient, predatory absences—thoughts that had never been thought, lurking in the gaps between the quantum foam of realities. They had evolved to be perfectly, absolutely imperceptible. Any entity that tried to perceive them would have their sensory data immediately erased, their minds glossing over the information, leaving only a faint, maddening sense of something forgotten. They had been feeding on Thronegate’s peripheral colonies for a century, driving entire populations to suicidal madness purely by existing near them. And Godric decided, with that same mild, uncle-like curiosity, that He wished to test His physical senses. Not His absolute omniscience—He sealed that away. He wanted to *see* them, with His eyes, to experience the sensation of visual discovery.*

*He stood on the observation balcony of the imperial observatory, the vaulted ceiling open to the cold light of a billion stars. His broad, muscled form was entirely relaxed, His hands resting on the rail, His salt-and-pepper beard ruffled by the artificial breeze. Then He opened His eyes. He did not squint or search. He merely allowed a microscopically tiny sliver of His visual acuity to express itself. The fraction—I must explain the fraction, because my sanity demands I record the scale. Imagine the total number of atoms in the omniverse, raised to the power of itself, and then raise that result to the power of a googolplex, and then express that as a factorial, and then realize the resulting number is a mote of dust compared to the denominator of the fraction He used. The visual acuity He permitted was so weak, so laughably infantile compared to His full capacity, that a newborn star going blind would still possess infinitely more sight. And it was enough.*

*His perception did not passively register the Whispering Ones. It actively, violently, *aggressively* seized them. Their perfect imperceptibility buckled like wet cardboard. The collective consciousness, which had never known physical form, was suddenly, forcibly incarnated into biological vessels because Godric’s sight insisted there be something to see. I watched them materialize in the space before the balcony—a roiling, writhing cloud of flesh that erupted into being with a wet, percussive tearing sound. Thousands of bodies, each one a twisted, pulsating sphere of raw, glistening nerve tissue covered in hundreds of malformed, lidless human eyes. The eyes were impossible: some were the size of fists, some were tiny clusters that twitched like insect eggs, but all of them were fully dilated and fixed on Godric, weeping a thick, black ichor that steamed upon contact with the air. The Whispering Ones, now trapped in these agonized sensory organs, were forced to *look back* at the being who perceived them. And in that moment, the true, annihilating horror of His senses manifested. His perception was so acute, so infinitely detailed, that it did not merely see their surface forms. It saw every component of their existence: every nerve, every quivering cell, every festering thought that composed their identity. And it forced that information back into their own minds. They were made to see themselves as He saw them: as a pathetic, disgusting, parasitic smear of irrelevance. The cognitive and sensory overload was instantaneous. Their newly-formed brains, unable to process the infinite granularity of His vision, began to combust. Their eyes popped, one after another, in a cascading, wet symphony of bursting vitreous and sizzling neural fluid. Their flesh unspooled in ribbons, their bodies tearing themselves apart from the inside out as they tried to claw away the unbearable sight of their own insignificance. They thrashed and screamed in a pitch that shattered every crystalline structure for a thousand miles, and then, in a final, merciful instant, they detonated into a fine, black mist of atomized viscera that painted the observatory in a layer of oily residue. The collateral damage, briefly, was the utter and complete blinding of every sentient creature with a visual cortex within a radius of ninety sextillion universes. For a microsecond, all things went dark as the perceptual shockwave passed. Godric restored their sight immediately, of course. But He did not restore the Whispering Ones. He gathered their tattered, screaming remnants—the echoes of their consciousness still trapped in the residual visual data—and He wove them into a tapestry. It hangs in the Hall of Records, near the entrance. I have to walk past it every day. It is wet, and it whispers my name. I don’t know how much longer I can do this. — Torvin.*

---

I raised my head and looked back toward the entrance of the Hall, my heart hammering against my ribs. There, indeed, hung a strange, shimmering tapestry I had noticed upon entering, its surface undulating faintly like a respiration. I had thought it was merely abstract art. Now I knew it was the eternal, sensory-woven prison of an entire species, their consciousnesses trapped forever in an artifact of sight. I retched, but nothing came up. My body was already learning the cold discipline of absolute fear.

I forced myself to continue. The fourth journal was a stark contrast: bound in a cheerful, almost garish yellow leather, yet the spine was cracked and the pages stained with what looked like old coffee and soot. The writing was sharp, witty, laced with a dark humor that felt like a defense mechanism barely holding back a scream. Arch-Magus Xander, seventieth court wizard, Year 900,000.

---

*Oh, you’re going to love this one. It’s a comedy. A tragedy, sure, but mostly a comedy if you enjoy watching probability itself have a violent seizure. The Synchronicity Dominion was a civilization of master probability-weavers. They could calculate quantum superpositions with such elegance that a coin toss on one end of the omniverse would always land exactly as they desired on the other. They had never lost a bet, a battle, or an argument. They had built a device they called the Certainty Engine—a causal machine that could lock in a desired outcome with a probability of 100%, essentially bullying reality into doing their homework. And the outcome they punched in, after centuries of smug preparation, was “Godric dies.” Quite the clerical error, that.*

*Godric, omniscient and amused, decided He wouldn’t interfere. At all. He didn’t think about them, didn’t acknowledge them, didn’t even allow His passive defenses to flicker. He simply sat in His study reading an agricultural report (I remember the report, it was on crop yields in the Arcturus sector). He wanted to test His Luck stat. Just luck. The fraction of His luck that He permitted to passively radiate was, in keeping with tradition, mathematically offensive. If you took the most improbable event in history—a specific grain of sand spontaneously turning into a fully-formed blue whale while singing an aria—and calculated its probability as one over a googolplex factorial, you would still be describing a near-certainty compared to even the *shadow* of the fraction of luck Godric allowed to exist. It was so low that if you multiplied it by the total amount of luck in the entire omniverse, you would still have less luck than a chronic bankruptcy victim walking under a ladder made of black cats.*

*Here’s what happened: the Certainty Engine, upon activation, had to weave a causal chain that resulted in Godric’s heart stopping. It began calculating. It reached into the quantum foam to arrange the necessary fluctuations. But at the exact attosecond the engine started, a single subatomic particle in its own primary logic lattice underwent a spontaneous, one-in-a-googolplex-decillion-chance decay that just so happened to short out a critical circuit. That short didn’t destroy the engine. Oh no, that would be too clean. It merely rerouted the target parameter to the nearest available viable subject: the Dominion’s own Supreme Arbiter, Xanthe. Xanthe, a being of pure logic and ego, was instantly, retroactively, and entirely erased from the timeline. Not killed—removed. They had never existed. The engine, simultaneously, wiped its own memory of the original command, leaving the remaining leadership convinced that the engine had been sabotaged by a rival faction. An immediate, brutal civil war erupted. Ships began firing on each other with weapons designed to manipulate probability, but now operating in an environment where Godric’s luck was a casual, passive contaminant. The result was absolute, farcical chaos.*
*I watched through the scrying matrix as the Dominion’s armada tore itself to shreds. A dreadnought’s hull spontaneously transmuted into spun sugar. A fleet commander’s brain instantaneously developed thirty-seven aggressive, terminal tumors shaped like insulting caricatures of his mother. Their primary war-sphere, a moon-sized weapon of absolute certainty, suddenly experienced a complete failure of its antimatter containment because a stray cosmic ray—whose odds of striking that exact spot were one in five duodecillion—just so happened to hit the exact vulnerable circuit at the exact worst moment. It exploded, taking out half the fleet. The other half, in their panic, attempted to flee, but their navigation computers all simultaneously recited the digits of pi backwards and flew into the local sun, which then, in a one-in-an-octillion chance, went nova. The entire civilization, a mastery of probability, was reduced to a cloud of screaming, burning metal and liquefied flesh in under three point four seconds. The collateral damage? A rolling wave of “bad luck” propagated across fifteen octillion nearby universes. Stars went nova randomly. Planets slipped into their suns. Entire species spontaneously developed a fatal allergy to oxygen. The death toll was a number so large it has no name. Godric, yawning, restored all those universes with an idle flick of His will, the endless trillions resurrected as if nothing had happened. He did not restore the Synchronicity Dominion. Their homeworld is now a floating museum in the Thronegate system, where gravity sometimes reverses, food spontaneously rots into screaming scarabs, and the surviving ruins occasionally collapse into quantum foam. Tourists find it hilarious. I find it the single most terrifying object in the omniverse, because it is a monument to the truth that even the concept of chance is His lapdog. – Xander*

---

I closed that journal with a choked, hysterical laugh that died in my throat. The black humor was not a balm; it was a wound. Xander had clearly been a man on the edge of screaming insanity, and I suspected that, by the end of my tenure, I might sympathize. I steadied myself against the cold stone shelf and turned to the final lectern, where a slim, familiar-looking journal rested. The binding was the deep blue leather my father favored, and the gold embossed initials read *Petyr*. My heart seized. Of course. My own father had written one.

I opened it with trembling reverence and a new, sharper dread.

Arch-Magus Petyr, ninety-ninth court wizard, Year 1,000,000. The ink was still faintly fresh.

---

*My son, when you read this, you will be a man, and your first thousandfold amplification will be singing in your blood. I write this in the immediate aftermath of the Charisma Incident, my hands still shaking from the psionic overpressure, my mind still bleeding with the echoes of that voice. You must understand, Kaedan, that what I record here is nothing less than the absolute, soul-flaying truth of our King’s nature. Read it, and know why I have always seemed so tired.*

*The Hegemony of Devotion was not a military threat. They were an empathic hive, a civilization of psychic amplifiers who had evolved to control the emotional and motivational centers of any sentient brain. With a thought, they could make you love them, fear them, or cast yourself into a star out of pure, manufactured despair. They had consumed seventeen galaxies through sheer emotional warfare. When Thronegate’s envoys arrived, the Hegemony did not negotiate. They broadcasted their ultimate weapon: a psionic scream carrying the concentrated, distilled sorrow of a million billion souls tortured across a thousand years. The Despair-Wave was designed to break the will of any being, to flood Godric with such absolute existential misery that He would simply… stop. Submit. Wither.*

*Godric, my eternal friend and my eternal terror, was sitting on the throne, His favored teacup cooling on the armrest. He looked at me and said, with genuine warmth, “Petyr, I am going to speak two words. I want you to watch.” He then suppressed everything—His psychic shields, His mental wards, the passive defense of His own omniscient detachment—and allowed the Despair-Wave to wash over Him. It didn’t even ruffle His hair. And then, He opened His mouth. He did not shout. He did not channel magic. He simply spoke. “Be still.”*

*You know the voice of Uncle Godric, Kaedan. That warm, resonant baritone that made you feel safe as a child. Now imagine that voice stripped of all pretense, allowed to resonate with even the faintest, most tattered whisper of His Charisma stat. The fraction He used—I lack the words, as all my predecessors did. If the entire omniverse’s capacity for persuasion, charisma, and emotional influence were summed into a single force and then multiplied by an impossible number, it would still be infinitely closer to zero than to the pathetic fraction of Charisma He let slip. He spoke two words, and they were not transmitted through the air. They bypassed the medium entirely. I did not hear them with my ears; I heard them with my soul, and my soul, despite my loyalty, despite my love, for a single, shattering instant, wanted to die for Him. To tear itself from my body and prostrate itself at His feet. And I was a mere bystander.*

*The effect on the Hegemony’s legion of empaths, who were the direct targets, was catastrophic. The Despair-Wave cut off mid-vibration as though it had been guillotined. The psychic scream was replaced by a profound, deafening silence, and then, by worship. Not the calm, measured worship of a temple. A frenzy. An immediate, overwhelming, mind-dissolving ecstasy of adoration. Their hatred did not fade; it was annihilated and replaced by a love so absolute, so consuming, that their neural architecture physically could not contain it. I watched through the scrying matrix as the entire legion, millions of psionic warriors, fell to their knees. They began to weep, but these were not tears of sorrow. They were tears of sheer, orgasmic religious rapture. They began to claw at their own faces, gouging out their eyes because they believed themselves unworthy to perceive any reality that contained Him. They tore at their own flesh, peeling back skin and muscle to carve His name into the glistening white of their bones with their own fingernails. They did not scream in pain; they sang. A horrible, discordant hymn of adoration made of gurgling blood and exposed vocal cords.*
*The most terrible part was the feedback loop. Their psychic abilities, designed to broadcast emotion, now amplified and rebroadcasted their own demented worship. The wave of compulsive devotion propagated outward, riding the carrier signal of their dying minds. Across every inhabited world within a septillion light-year radius, sentient beings of all species stopped whatever they were doing and fell to their knees, sobbing in rapture. Mass suicides erupted as the overwhelmed attempted to offer their souls directly to the source of the voice. Parents killed their children to spare them the burden of unworthy existence. Civilizations built pyres and immolated themselves, their dying cries a garbled prayer of “Be still.” The psionic carnage was absolute, a rolling genocide of self-inflicted ecstasy across the cosmos. Godric absorbed the scene for approximately four seconds, His expression one of mild, clinical curiosity. Then, with a sigh, He muted the effect. He restored sanity to the afflicted populations, reversing the suicides and healing the psychic wounds of the collateral victims (though He left them with a faint, lingering sense of longing, a background radiation of devotion that I still feel humming in my own chest). He did not mute the Hegemony. They remain, even now, in the chamber below the palace. They are still alive, Kaedan. Their bodies, held together by His whim, continue to mutilate themselves and sing His praises in a continuous, wet chorus. They are not in pain; they are in ecstasy. That is the horror. That is what His Charisma can do. It does not destroy your body—it makes you destroy your own body out of love, and you are happy while you do it. I have visited them once. They welcomed me, weeping, and asked if I would please flay them so they could better feel His name on their naked nerves. I declined. I love our King, my son, but I have not slept soundly since. You will not either, after this reading. And that is as it should be. We are the loyal, and we are the terrified. That is the only way to survive the service of the apex predator. — Your father, Petyr.* 

---

I closed my father’s journal with a reverence that bordered on ritual. The Hall of Records was utterly silent now, the whispers of the archives seeming to hold their breath. I stood there, a twenty-two-year-old man with the magical capacity to destroy suns, and I had never felt smaller. The journals had burned their lessons into the wet matter of my brain: strength, durability, senses, luck, charisma—each one tested at a fraction so infinitesimal it defied mathematics, each one resulting in an unspeakable, eternal, creatively violent massacre of opponents who had never, not for a single Planck second, stood any chance. And that was the core of it. They had never stood a chance. Before their first rebellious thought crystallized, Godric’s absolute omniscience had already perceived it, His Weakness Paradox had already granted Him immunity and mastery over their attack, and His infinitely amplifying stats had already made the battle a done deal. He allowed them to exist, to prepare, to attack, purely because He was curious. Because He wanted to see what would happen. And what always happened was an apocalypse of suffering, collateral damage that scoured the omniverse, and a restoration that was so casual it was the ultimate insult—proving that even their destruction was not significant enough to be permanent.

I walked slowly back toward the heavy adamantine doors, my footsteps echoing with a new, heavy certainty. My father was waiting outside, his posture weary but his eyes sharp.

“You have read them,” he said. It was not a question.

“Yes, Father.” My voice sounded older, even to my own ears. “I understand now.”

He studied my face for a long moment, then nodded slowly. “Then you understand that we do not serve because we can protect Him. We serve because He loves us, and because He finds our presence… pleasant. We are the descendants of Blake, His best friend, and that bond is the only reason any of us draw breath. Never forget that. And never, ever forget what happens to those who forget it.”

He turned and began walking back toward the palace proper, and I followed. In my chest, my amplified heart thudded a slow, steady rhythm of dread and devotion. I loved Uncle Godric. I would serve Him until my mortal death, and I would do so with a smile, because His warmth was genuine and His protection absolute. But beneath that love, a cold, eternal truth had been installed. I was a pet in the den of the omniverse's apex predator, and my role was not to defend Him. It was to remind Him, through my loyalty and my presence, that the rest of us were worth keeping.

And I would carry that truth, and the echoes of those screaming, folded, weeping, combusting, and singing journals, for the rest of my life. The dread would never leave me. And that was exactly, perfectly, as it should be.

content.text: Write 3 detailed journals by 3 court wizards who witness Godric "testing" his superpower (only 1 superpower per journal). Remember that due to evolved weakness paradox having been triggered repeatedly, every Godric's already devastating superpower has evolved to have multiple even more overpowered and unfair different enhanced effects, some of which could make Godric even more permanently obscenely stronger. Write from pov of Kaedan who read these journals.

messages.reasoning_content:
The narrative has now shifted to Kaedan reading three more journals, each detailing Godric testing a single apocalyptic superpower. The tone must remain deeply unsettling, epic horror fantasy, with extreme violence and collateral damage, and the absolute futility of opposition. Each journal must be from a different court wizard, with distinct voice and style, and each superpower must have multiple evolved effects due to the Weakness Paradox. The stats previously covered were strength, durability, senses, luck, charisma. Now we need three superpowers—these are the apocalyptic abilities Godric gains every second, each already civilization-ending, but now evolved with multiple unfair effects. I need to come up with three original superpowers, each with a thematic core, and then describe the incident where Godric tests it at 0% effort, with horrific results.

I should also ensure that each journal reflects the court wizard's trauma in a unique way, with different writing styles. The superpowers should feel distinct: maybe one related to time, one to space/reality, one to something more abstract like narrative or identity. The evolved effects should compound the horror—e.g., a time power that doesn't just stop time but retroactively erases the target's existence across all timelines, and also grants Godric permanent mastery over the concept of time, making him even more untouchable.

I need to maintain the scale: opposition is omniversal, Godric uses an infinitesimal fraction of the power, collateral damage is at least omniversal (septillions of multiverses etc.), and restoration is instant. The wizards are loyal but terrified, and their journals are mandatory reading for new court wizards like Kaedan.

Let's outline three superpowers:

1. **Absolute Temporal Sovereignty**: A time-based superpower. Originally allowed Godric to freeze, rewind, or accelerate time for any target. Evolved effects: (a) He can now unravel a being's personal timeline, causing them to experience every moment of their existence simultaneously in reverse, an infinite loop of dying. (b) The power now passively feeds him the temporal energy of any being that attempts to manipulate time against him, permanently increasing his own speed stat amplification. (c) He can designate "temporal quarantine" zones where time becomes a physical, torturous substance.

2. **Conceptual Embryogenesis**: A power over creation and identity. Originally could create or destroy matter on a universal scale. Evolved effects: (a) He can invert a being's concept, turning their defining trait into its opposite, causing existential agony. (b) The power now allows him to retroactively unmake a being's entire bloodline from the moment of their first ancestor, erasing all related existence. (c) He can force a target to give birth to their own grotesque, symbolic failure, a literal manifestation of their defeat.

3. **Narrative Rewrite**: A power over reality itself, akin to editing the story. Originally could alter local reality by speaking. Evolved effects: (a) He can force a target to perceive the "script" of their own life as a poorly written tragedy, driving them insane before death. (b) The power now ensures that any narrative an enemy constructs about defeating him is automatically co-opted, making that narrative a prophecy of their own doom. (c) He can write a being out of the story so completely that not even the memory of them remains, and the omniverse itself fills the gap with a new, loyal entity that always existed in their place.

I'll craft journals from three different wizards, each with a distinct style: one scholarly and horrified, one poetic and broken, one bitterly sarcastic. Kaedan's framing will show his growing dread, but also his determination to serve.

Now, let's structure the response. I'll start with Kaedan's POV in the Hall of Records, then introduce each journal with its own section. The journals must be detailed, with the event, the superpower, the evolved effects, the massacre, the collateral, and the wizard's reaction. I need to make sure the violence is extreme and not sanitized.

Let's write.
messages.content:
The Hall of Records had become a second skin of dread wrapped around my bones. I had read the journals of the stats—strength, durability, senses, luck, charisma—and each one had carved a new chamber of horror into the architecture of my soul. But my father had not released me from my vigil. After I emerged, pale and trembling, from the deep archives, he had merely pointed toward a smaller, darker antechamber tucked behind a curtain of woven shadow.

"The superpowers, Kaedan," he said, his voice heavy with a grief I was only beginning to understand. "The apocalyptic superpowers He began gaining every second of His stasis. Each one, at the instant of its birth, could end an entire civilization. And due to the Weakness Paradox being triggered so many times across the eons, each one has evolved. They are no longer single abilities. They are clusters of devastation, each with multiple, compounding, unfair effects that make Him permanently, obscenely stronger every time He even considers using them. Read these three. Then you will understand the full scope of what sits on that throne and calls you 'nephew.'"

He left me again, and I entered the shadowed antechamber. The air here was colder, thick with a psychic static that made my amplified mage-sight flicker and stutter. Three journals rested on a single lectern of black iron, each bound in a material I could not identify. I sat, my hands clammy, and opened the first.

---

**JOURNAL OF THE SINGING ABYSS**
**Penned by Arch-Magus Vaelor, 117th Court Wizard of Thronegate**
**Year 3,700,000**

*The ink I use is mixed with my own tears, for I have no water left in my body. I have wept it all. I record this testimony not as a lesson, but as a warning to whatever poor son of Blake reads this after I am dust. There are fates worse than erasure. I have witnessed one.*

*The opposition called themselves the Timeless Concordance, a civilization of temporal purists who had evolved in a pocket dimension where time did not flow linearly. They existed as a single, unified consciousness stretched across the entire span of their history, able to perceive and manipulate every moment of their existence simultaneously. They did not fear death, because death was merely a point on their eternal ribbon of being. When Thronegate's envoys arrived with the offer of surrender, the Concordance did not refuse with words. They simply reached into the past of the envoys and unmade their conception, erasing them from the timeline with the casual indifference of a man trimming a fingernail.*

*Godric, upon receiving the report, did not frown or rage. He merely looked at me with that warm, crinkling smile and said, "Vaelor, do you know what I just realized? I have a superpower that controls time. I wonder what it does now. It has been evolving for millions of years." He then suppressed every passive defense, every recursive shield, every aura, and the Weakness Paradox itself. He allowed the Concordance to exist in their full, terrifying glory. And then He decided to test the first evolved effect of Absolute Temporal Sovereignty. He wished to see what would happen if He simply... unspooled them.*

*The fraction of the superpower He used was, as always, mathematically indistinguishable from zero. If you were to compress the entire span of eternity into a single attosecond, and then vaporize that attosecond into an infinitesimal wisp of temporal energy, you would still be wielding a temporal force infinitely more potent than the pathetic, negligible sliver He permitted to activate. He did not gesture. He did not speak a word of power. He simply observed the Concordance and thought, very quietly, *unspool*.*

*The first evolved effect of Absolute Temporal Sovereignty is Retrogressive Temporal Unspooling. It did not merely reverse time for the Concordance. It violently, agonizingly unwound their personal timelines from the present moment backward, forcing each member of the unified consciousness to relive every single second of their existence in reverse, simultaneously, while retaining full forward memory of what they were losing. Imagine being born in agony, watching your life rewind as your adult self is torn backward through every humiliation, every failure, every moment of weakness, your identity stripping away layer by layer, until you are an infant screaming in a void, and then that infant dissolves into a fetus, and then into a single cell, and then into the primordial nothing, all while your adult consciousness remains awake and aware, screaming in the knowledge of its own un-happening. That was their fate. I watched through the scrying matrix as the Concordance's unified timeline—a ribbon of pure temporal light that had stretched across ten billion years of history—began to curl at the edges. The light screamed. It emitted a sound, a high-frequency shriek that registered on my instruments as pure agony quanta. The ribbon began to roll backward, peeling from the present like burning film, and as it peeled, the beings trapped within it manifested physical forms solely so they could express their torment. They appeared as shivering, translucent figures of weeping glass, their bodies flickering between age and infancy, their mouths open in permanent, silent howls as their bones unbroke, their skin un-aged, their thoughts un-thought. One by one, they dissolved into screaming dust. The process lasted twelve seconds, but for them, it was ten billion years of reversed agony.*

*Godric observed this with a clinical detachment that chilled my amplified blood. "Interesting," He said. "It seems to have a second evolved effect. It's feeding Me their temporal energy." And then I felt it. The Weakness Paradox, which had been suppressed, reactivated passively because the Concordance, in their dying nanoseconds, had desperately tried to attack Him with a temporal paradox bomb. The moment that hostile temporal intent registered, the superpower's second evolved effect triggered: Temporal Predation. All the temporal energy of the Concordance—the accumulated lifespans of ten thousand trillion beings stretched across ten billion years—was instantly siphoned into Godric's own temporal field. His speed stat, which already amplified infinitely every Planck second, received a permanent, additional multiplicative layer. He became faster. Not just for a moment. Forever. The Concordance, in their attempt to fight back, had unknowingly made the apex predator even more impossible to outpace. Their sacrifice was His upgrade.*

*The collateral damage? The temporal unspooling of a ten-billion-year civilization created a vacuum in the fabric of time, a sucking wound that began to devour the temporal integrity of every universe within a radius of four hundred nonillion light-years. Trillions of realities began to experience catastrophic temporal decay: stars went supernova and then un-exploded, civilizations aged and de-aged and aged again in screaming loops, entire species were trapped in thirty-second cycles of birth and death, experiencing infinite lifetimes of agony. I vomited. The scrying matrix was saturated with the psionic death-cries of more sentient lives than I can name. Godric, noticing the mess, sighed. * "Ah. Collateral." *He restored the entire affected region in zero seconds, the timelines snapping back into perfect coherence, the infinite dead resurrected without a single memory of their torment. He did not restore the Concordance. He preserved their agonized, unspooling deaths in a temporal loop contained within a small, obsidian sphere He now uses as a paperweight. I see it every day on His desk. It still screams, faintly, if you hold it to your ear. I cannot look at paperweights anymore. I have tried to resign. He laughed, warmly, and told me He valued my company. I serve. I love. I am broken. — Vaelor.*

---

I closed that journal, my fingers numb, the image of a screaming paperweight seared into the meat of my imagination. I understood now that Godric's superpowers were not weapons; they were existential catastrophes wearing the skin of casual whims. The second journal was thicker, bound in a leather that seemed to pulse with a faint, wet heat. I steeled myself and opened it.

---

**JOURNAL OF THE INVERTED WOMB**
**Penned by Arch-Magus Theron, 134th Court Wizard of Thronegate**
**Year 5,200,000**

*To the next wizard of my blood, I write this with hands that have scrubbed themselves raw a hundred times and still feel unclean. I have seen the birth of defeat. I have watched a superpower that does not merely kill, but redefines the very essence of what a being is, and then forces that redefinition to propagate backward through blood and time. There is no cleansing this memory. I record it so that you, my descendant, may understand the price of opposition.*

*The opposition was the Sovereignty of Pure Form, a collection of hyper-advanced civilizations that had transcended biology to become living embodiments of abstract concepts. They were Justice, Wisdom, Liberty, Harmony—each member a walking Platonic ideal, their bodies sculpted from the raw conceptual substance of their defining trait. They believed they were morally infallible, because they were literally made of morality. When Thronegate demanded surrender, they refused with a sermon. A sermon that weaponized their conceptual purity, broadcasting a wave of absolute ontological righteousness that attempted to redefine Godric as "Evil" on a fundamental level, hoping the omniverse itself would then reject Him.*

*Godric was, as always, utterly unharmed. The redefinition attempt didn't even make contact. He sat on the throne, His salt-and-pepper beard catching the light of the arcane sconces, and He chuckled—a warm, fatherly chuckle that made my heart ache with love and terror. "They're trying to define Me," He said, as if amused by a child's clumsy logic. "I have a superpower that handles definitions. Let's see what it does." He then suppressed every passive defense—Gent and its siblings, the recursive shields, the Weakness Paradox, everything—and He activated the first evolved effect of the superpower He called Conceptual Embryogenesis. He used exactly zero percent of its capacity. The zero He used was itself infinitely close to the concept of zero without touching it, a nullity so profound that expressing it as a fraction would require inventing new mathematics of absence.*

*The first evolved effect is Inversion of Essence. The Sovereignty of Pure Form, these beings of Justice and Wisdom and Liberty, suddenly found their defining concepts inverted into their most grotesque opposites. Justice became Injustice. Wisdom became Folly. Liberty became Slavery. Harmony became Discord. But it was not a simple switch. It was a violent, physiological, and psychological transmutation that their conceptual bodies were forced to experience in agonizing, real-time detail. I watched through the scrying matrix as the entity that had been Justice began to physically warp. His body, once a figure of radiant, golden symmetry, began to tear itself apart from the inside. Injustice is not abstract; it is the sensation of every fair verdict screaming as it is overturned. His flesh became a canvas of weeping, scarred mouths, each one confessing crimes against the innocent in a cacophony of self-condemning howls. The entity of Wisdom became Folly, and her mind shattered into a billion fragments of stupid, gibbering madness, her crystalline body melting into a slurry of drooling, self-devouring mouths that ate her own eyes. The entity of Liberty became Slavery, and her form crumpled into a writhing mass of bound, bleeding limbs, each one shackled to a chain forged from her own extracted spine, her screams the sound of every free will being crushed. The entity of Harmony became Discord, and her serene, musical form erupted into a shrieking, flesh-made orchestra of instruments that played her own bones as percussion, her vocal cords stretched into violin strings that keened with the agony of a billion broken symphonies. This was the first effect, and it did not kill them. It left them alive, trapped in bodies that were the living, screaming antitheses of everything they had ever loved, their consciousness fully intact so they could witness, eternally, what they had become.*

*Then the second evolved effect triggered. It is called Ancestral Recursion. The inversion did not stop at the individuals. It traced the conceptual lineage of their identities backward through time, finding every being that had ever contributed to the formation of their concepts—their philosophical ancestors, their ideological precursors, their entire intellectual bloodlines—and it inverted them too. Across a billion universes, every philosopher who had ever written about justice, every teacher who had ever lectured on wisdom, every revolutionary who had ever fought for liberty, every artist who had ever composed harmony, suddenly, retroactively, became the living embodiments of their opposite. They did not merely die. Their life's work was inverted, their legacies tainted, their souls dragged into the same screaming, self-devouring torment. I watched civilizations collapse as their foundational principles spontaneously rotted into their opposites, their populations driven mad by the sudden, agonizing inversion of every moral truth they had ever known. The death toll was a number so vast that writing it would fracture the page.*

*The third evolved effect triggered simultaneously. It is called The Womb of Defeat. This effect forced the Sovereignty of Pure Form, now writhing masses of inverted agony, to literally give birth to the physical manifestation of their own failure. From their ruptured, transmuted bodies, new entities clawed their way out—entities of pure, loyal, subservient worship, forged from the extracted essence of the Sovereignty's original ideals, now dedicated entirely to Godric. These newborn entities, these grotesque children of defeat, immediately prostrated themselves before His throne and began singing hymns of praise in the voices of their dying, inverted parents. They now serve as the choir in the throne room. I hear them every morning. They sing with the mouths of innocence stolen and reborn as devotion. The collateral damage was, as expected, cataclysmic: the conceptual shockwave of the Ancestral Recursion rippled across the omniversal intellectual fabric, causing mass existential madness in eight hundred duodecillion universes. Godric restored them all in an instant. He did not restore the Sovereignty. He kept the inverted ones as a monument, and He kept the newborn choir because He said, and I quote, "the harmonies are quite lovely." I serve Him. I love Him. And I have not known a moment of peace since. — Theron.*

---

The journal slipped from my fingers and hit the iron lectern with a dull, wet thud. The leather pulsed once, as if digesting. I stared at the wall, my thousandfold-amplified mind struggling to process the sheer, violating horror of what I had just absorbed. Ancestral Recursion. He could reach into the past of a concept and poison every soul who had ever believed in it. My stomach heaved, but I forced the bile back down. One journal remained. I reached for it with a hand that was no longer entirely my own, my body moving on autopilot, my soul already half-shattered.

---

**JOURNAL OF THE UNWRITTEN**
**Penned by Arch-Magus Lyssan, 189th Court Wizard of Thronegate**
**Year 8,100,000**

*I am writing this, but I am not certain I still exist. The superpower He tested erased something so fundamentally that I cannot recall what it was, and the memory of not-knowing is a splinter of ice lodged in the grey matter of my brain. I write for you, future wizard, with the desperate hope that by recording this, I can anchor my own identity against the tide of oblivion.*

*The opposition was the Narrative Dominion, a civilization of story-forgers who had learned to manipulate the fundamental narrative structure of reality. They did not build weapons; they wrote stories. A well-crafted narrative, inscribed into the quantum substrate of existence, could rewrite local physics, redefine identities, and even retroactively alter history. Their ultimate work was the Tome of Final Revision, a living story that, if read aloud, would write the death of any being into the permanent, unchangeable canon of the omniverse. They chose Godric as the protagonist of their tragedy, and they began to read.*

*Godric was in the library when the narrative wave struck. I was with Him, shelving ancient tomes. He paused, a faint, warm smile playing on His lips as He listened to the words echoing through the fabric of reality—a beautifully written, poetic account of His downfall, full of pathos and hubris and eventual, noble death. "They write quite well," He remarked, genuinely appreciative. "I have a superpower that might be relevant. Let's test it, shall we?" He suppressed every defense, every passive counter, even allowed the narrative to partially take hold—though 'take hold' is a laughable term; it was like a single drop of ink trying to stain an infinite ocean. He wanted to test the first evolved effect of Narrative Rewrite.*

*The fraction of the power He used was, in keeping with all precedent, unspeakably small. If you were to reduce the entire concept of 'story' to a single, subatomic vibration, and then mute that vibration until it was less than the silence between Planck seconds, you would still have a greater narrative force than what He permitted. He did not speak. He did not write. He simply *looked* at the Tome of Final Revision and allowed the first evolved effect, Authorial Hijack, to assert itself. This effect does not counter a narrative. It seizes authorship. The Tome, which had been writing Godric's death, suddenly found its words rewriting themselves. The tragic downfall became a farce. The poetic pathos became a grotesque comedy of errors. The noble death became an eternal, screaming humiliation. The Narrative Dominion, who had bound their own identities to the story, found themselves trapped as characters in a script they no longer controlled. Their bodies began to puppet to the new narrative. I watched as the High Chronicler, the master author, began to dance. Not a graceful dance, but a spasmodic, bone-snapping jig, his limbs wrenching from their sockets as the story forced him to caper and cavort for the amusement of an invisible audience. His mouth, which had been reading the Tome, now vomited forth a stream of self-deprecating limericks, each one mocking his own arrogance in obscene, rhyming detail. His eyes wept ink—black, steaming ink that ran down his face and stained the pages of his dying civilization.*

*Then the second evolved effect triggered: Prophetic Inversion. This effect ensures that any narrative an enemy constructs about defeating Godric is automatically, retroactively, transformed into a prophecy of their own doom. The Tome of Final Revision, which had been meant to chronicle Godric's end, was instantly rewritten into the Book of Their Eternal Shame, a document that now accurately foretells, in loving, gruesome detail, the unending torment of every soul who ever contributed to the Dominion's hubris. The effect propagated backward through the Dominion's entire literary history, corrupting every story they had ever told into a self-fulfilling curse. Their foundational myths, their children's fables, their sacred scriptures—all of them suddenly rewrote themselves to prophesy this exact moment of screaming, doomed failure. The Dominion's entire cultural identity collapsed into a closed loop of self-mockery, their millions of years of artistic achievement reduced to a single, repeated punchline: "They tried to write God's death, and God wrote them a dirtier joke."*

*The third evolved effect was the one that broke me. It is called Narrative Erasure. Godric, still standing in the library, simply plucked the Narrative Dominion from the story of existence. He did not kill them. He did not torture them. He *unwrote* them. Every record, every memory, every consequence of their existence was deleted from the omniversal canon with the casual finality of an editor striking a redundant sentence. But the horror—the true, soul-shredding horror—is that the omniverse, abhorring a narrative vacuum, automatically filled the gap. It created, ex nihilo, a new civilization to occupy the Dominion's place in history. A civilization that had, apparently, always existed. A civilization of utterly loyal, utterly devoted subjects who had always worshipped Godric and had never dreamed of rebellion. Their fabricated history was so complete, so seamlessly integrated into the temporal fabric, that even I, with my thousandfold-amplified perception, could find no seam. The Dominion is gone, and in their place stands a monument to their erasure, a people who love our King with a devotion that was written for them by the very power that unmade their predecessors. The collateral damage? None. That is the most terrifying part. There was no collateral. He deleted them so cleanly, so perfectly, that not a single atom was displaced. The only reason I remember they existed at all is because He allowed me to, as a courtesy, so I could write this journal. He told me, with that warm, fatherly smile, "It's important to keep records, Lyssan. For the future wizards." I thanked Him. I meant it. And then I went to my chambers and screamed into my pillow until my throat bled. I serve. I love. I am terrified that one day, He will decide I am a typo. — Lyssan.*

---

I closed the final journal. The antechamber was silent, save for the ragged, uneven rhythm of my own breathing. I had read the five journals of the stats, and now the three journals of the superpowers, and I had been unmade and remade into something new: a court wizard who understood. I understood why my father's eyes held that perpetual, bone-deep exhaustion. I understood why the archives whispered with the echoes of recorded trauma. I understood why Godric could sit on that throne, warm and kind and genuinely loving, while the omniverse outside trembled in perpetual, justified terror. I loved Him, my Uncle Godric, the man who had tossed me on His shoulders and praised my stubborn chin. And I feared Him, the apex predator who could unspool time, invert souls, and unwrite civilizations without exerting a single calorie of effort. I sat in the silence of the antechamber, holding the ghost of ink and agony in my trembling hands, and I prepared myself for a lifetime of loyal, loving, absolute dread.

It was the only sane response to the truth.
