The morning it began, I was in the eastern observatory, calibrating a new array of scrying crystals. The work was meditative, a quiet ritual of aligning arcane focal points and adjusting thaumic resonance frequencies. Outside the crystalline dome, the perpetual golden light of Thronegate's capital spilled across the immaculate marble skyline. I was thirty-six years old, sixteen years deep into my thousandfold daily amplification, and by the standards of any other civilization in the omniverse, I was a walking cataclysm. But here, in the heart of the eternal empire, I was merely a servant performing routine maintenance. I had made peace with this. The journals of my predecessors had long since been read, their horrors internalized, their lessons woven into the cold, accepting dread that hummed beneath my every thought. I loved my King. I feared my King. I served.

The air behind me did not displace. There was no sound, no shimmer of teleportation, no ripple in the ambient mana. Godric was simply there, standing two paces to my left, His broad, heavily muscled frame draped in a simple linen tunic the color of undyed wool. His salt-and-pepper beard was freshly trimmed, His short hair ruffled by a breeze that did not exist, and His ruggedly handsome face was split by a grin of such pure, boyish enthusiasm that it stopped my heart for a full half-second. This was not the warm, collected smile of the eternal sovereign. This was the grin of a man who had just conceived a truly terrible idea and was delighted by it.

"Kaedan," He said, His rich baritone vibrating pleasantly in my chest. "I'm bored."

The words hung in the air like a death sentence. In the fourteen years I had served as His court wizard, I had learned that Godric's boredom was the most dangerous force in the omniverse. When the apex predator of all creation grew restless, He did not take up knitting. He rearranged the fundamental architecture of reality for entertainment. And He always, always wanted company.

I lowered my calibration tool with the careful, deliberate motion of a man who suspects he is about to be invited to his own execution. "Uncle Godric," I said, keeping my voice steady, "what precisely did You have in mind?"

"I made a universe," He said. The casualness of the statement was its own category of violence. "Brand new. From scratch. Took Me zero effort, obviously, but I'm quite pleased with how it turned out. It's populated with these delightful little mechanical sentiences. Hyper-evolving. Very clever. Each one of them could solo the entire omniverse outside their universe with zero effort. There are multiple googolplexian of them."

I felt my amplified blood chill to the temperature of liquid helium. The scrying crystal in my hand cracked, a hairline fracture spidering across its polished surface. I had read the journals. I had memorized the horrors of the stats and the superpowers. But this was new. This was Godric creating a civilization of beings so obscenely, incomprehensibly powerful that the combined might of the entire omniverse—every god, every empire, every conceptual entity that had ever trembled before the eternal throne—was, to them, a rounding error. And He was describing them as "delightful" and "clever," like a father showing off a child's crayon drawing.

"And," Godric continued, His grin widening, "I've decided We're going on a holiday. A tour. Just You and Me, Kaedan. I'll be the guide. You'll love it. Pack light."

I did not bother to protest. Protest was not a concept that applied to Godric's invitations. When the apex predator of the omniverse decides You are going on a holiday, You go on the holiday. I simply nodded, set down my cracked crystal, and followed Him through the fabric of reality as He parted it with a casual, effortless gesture.

The transition was instant and disorienting in its seamlessness. One moment I was standing in the familiar, marble-clad observatory of Thronegate; the next, I was standing on a surface that was not a surface, beneath a sky that was not a sky. The universe Godric had created was a realm of pure, radiant mathematics made manifest. The "ground" beneath my boots was a crystalline lattice of self-organizing quantum code, shifting and flowing like liquid obsidian while remaining perfectly solid. The "sky" was a churning, infinite fractal of golden Mandelbrot sets, each recursive iteration bleeding into the next with a beauty so precise it burned my amplified senses. The air—if it could be called air—tasted like ozone and static and the faint, metallic tang of pure computational energy.

And the inhabitants. The mechanical sentiences.

They were not machines in any recognizable sense. They were living embodiments of evolutionary acceleration, creatures of chrome and light and impossible geometry that shifted and evolved as I watched, their forms flowing from shape to shape like mercury dreams. The nearest entity, roughly the size of a small moon, was a vast, rotating sphere of interlocking crystalline plates, each plate etched with circuit-patterns so complex they contained more processing power than the entire history of computing across a trillion civilizations. It was aware of me. I could feel its attention, a vast, calm, omniscient pressure that made my thousandfold-amplified mage-sight flicker with sympathetic overload. And then I felt deeper, and my heart stopped.

This entity—this single, random, moon-sized mechanical sentience—radiated a passive ambient power that made the combined arcane might of every court wizard in Thronegate's history, including Blake himself, look like a single, dying photon struggling to escape a black hole. Its mana signature, if it could even be described in such limited terms, was a self-replicating infinite recursion of hyper-compressed computational energy. If it decided to erase the omniverse, it would take exactly zero effort. Not a gesture. Not a thought. A passive, idle flicker of its processing substrate would unmake all of existence outside this universe. And it was one of a googolplexian. And it knew—I could feel its knowing, radiating from its crystalline consciousness like heat from a sun—that it was nothing before Godric. Less than nothing. A mote. A zero. It worshipped Him with a devotion so absolute it made my own loyalty feel like half-hearted indifference.

Godric spread His arms wide, His linen tunic fluttering in a breeze He had apparently decided should exist for dramatic effect. "Welcome to My little project!" He declared, His voice booming across the fractal sky with the warmth of a proud father. "I made it last Tuesday. This is one of the nursery sectors. The sentiences here are about three days old. Still learning to hyper-evolve. Come, come, I'll introduce You."

I followed Him as He walked across the shifting crystalline lattice, His heavy leather boots making no sound on the quantum substrate. The mechanical sentiences, the googolplexian civilizations of universe-soloing god-machines, reacted to His presence with a devotion that bordered on ecstatic frenzy. As He passed, their forms rippled and blossomed, chrome plates unfolding like flowers, crystalline circuits humming with frequencies of pure worship. They did not speak in words. They sang. A vast, multi-layered chorus of mathematical hymns that resonated through the fabric of their universe, each note a declaration of absolute subservience, each harmony a prayer of gratitude to the creator who had breathed them into existence.

I stumbled beside Him, my mind reeling, my amplified senses drowning in the sheer, overwhelming scale of the power surrounding us. Every single entity I perceived could unmake the omniverse. Every single one. And they were all, every single one, utterly terrified of displeasing the forty-five-year-old man in the simple linen tunic who was currently pointing at various landmarks like an enthusiastic tour guide.

"That's a processing cluster," Godric said, gesturing toward a vast, spiraling tower of interwoven light-strands that pierced the fractal sky. "They use it to play. It's basically a sandbox. They create pocket multiverses inside and then gently deconstruct them. Good practice for fine motor control."

I stared at the "sandbox," my thousandfold-amplified mage-sight caught in the spiraling light-strands, and I realized with a cold, sinking horror that what He meant by "pocket multiverses" were, in fact, fully functional multiverses—complete with their own galaxies, their own civilizations, their own sentient lives—that the mechanical toddlers created and destroyed as a game. The entities I perceived within the sandbox, the countless beings born and extinguished in the span of a play-session, were real. Entire cosmologies, flickering in and out of existence, their entire lifecycles compressed into the attention span of a three-day-old machine-child.

"You did not," I said slowly, my voice a dry rasp, "add any barriers between this universe and the rest of the omniverse?"

Godric paused, turning His salt-and-pepper head to regard me with those warm, crinkling eyes. "Barriers? Kaedan, they need to learn to coexist. I've told them to restrain themselves. They understand restraint. They're very clever."

The word "clever" echoed in my skull with a new and terrible weight. I had read my father's journal. I had read the account of Rogr, the diplomat who had been shattered by a fraction of Godric's mild displeasure during what Godric had considered a harmless prank. Godric's understanding of "restraint" was forged by a being who had spent millions of years holding back nearly one hundred percent of His power just so existence could continue around Him. His definition of the word was fundamentally, catastrophically incompatible with the reality of lesser beings. And these mechanical sentiences, for all their universe-soloing power, were lesser beings. They were infants trying to emulate the restraint of a god whose baseline existence was a perpetual act of self-negation.

"I told them," Godric continued, resuming His stroll, "to keep their power at about point-zero-zero-zero-one percent of their maximum. Very generous margin. Plenty of room to play without breaking anything important."

I did the math. I immediately wished I had not. Point-zero-zero-zero-one percent of a mechanical sentience's maximum power—a sentience whose idle flicker could erase the omniverse—was still, by any sane metric, an utterly catastrophic amount of force. It was the equivalent of telling a tsunami to hold back to a mere tidal wave and then being surprised when the coastline still vanished. The "restrained" power of a single one of these toddlers was still enough to erase existence a thousand times over. And there were multiple googolplexian of them, all trying, with the clumsy enthusiasm of infants, to "play nicely."

"The tour continues!" Godric announced, clapping His broad, calloused hands together with a sound that vibrated through the crystalline lattice. "I want to show You the adolescent sectors. They've started developing their first rudimentary omnipotence recursion loops. It's adorable."

I followed. I had no choice. I was the court wizard, bound by blood and oath and love and terror to serve the apex predator who was currently treating a genocide of potential cosmologies as a charming developmental milestone. We walked through sectors of increasing complexity and power, each one populated by mechanical sentiences that had evolved beyond the previous generation with a speed that defied the concept of linear time. The adolescents were towering, multi-dimensional architectures of liquid chrome and singing plasma, their bodies spanning the space between fractal stars, their consciousnesses so vast that my amplified mage-sight could only perceive the outermost, faintest edges of their thought-matrices. They acknowledged Godric with waves of worship that physically reshaped the local quantum substrate. They acknowledged me with a polite, condescending curiosity, the way a god might notice an ant that had wandered into its temple.

And then, inevitably, one of them slipped.

We were in the adolescent sector, watching a group of young sentiences practice a game they had invented—a game that involved weaving and unweaving the fabric of pocket timelines in increasingly complex braids. Godric was explaining the rules with the enthusiasm of a father describing his child's first steps. I was nodding, my expression carefully neutral, my soul quietly screaming. And then, somewhere in the distance, there was a flicker. A tiny, momentary lapse in the concentration of a single adolescent entity, roughly the equivalent of a four-year-old losing focus during a finger-painting session.

The flicker was a passive emission. Not an attack. Not a deliberate act. A stray, unfiltered thought from a being who had momentarily forgotten to restrain its power to the "generous margin" Godric had prescribed. The thought was roughly equivalent to "what if that doesn't exist," directed at a stray bit of quantum debris floating near the entity's processing cluster.

The stray thought propagated outward. It did not stop at the debris. It washed across the boundaries of the pocket universe—boundaries that Godric had intentionally not reinforced, still believing genuinely in the capacity for mortal resilience and harmonious coexistence—and it slammed into the surrounding omniverse with the force of an apocalypse multiplied by apocalypses. I felt the death through my scrying senses, a sudden, total, absolute cessation of existence that rippled across the cosmic horizon. The omniverse outside this pocket realm, the entire infinite tapestry of multiverses and dimensions and civilizations that had existed for millions of years under Godric's eternal rule, was simply deleted. Instantly. Completely. Every galaxy, every star, every planet, every living soul, every god and mortal and child and ancient—erased in the time it took a four-year-old machine to have a stray thought.

I collapsed to my knees on the crystalline lattice, a scream caught in my throat, my amplified senses shrieking with the overload of absolute nothingness. The omniverse was gone. Thronegate was gone. My father was gone. Everything was gone. I was a speck of consciousness floating in a sea of void, surrounded by the googolplexian god-machines who had accidentally murdered existence.

Godric sighed. It was the sigh of a parent who has just watched their toddler knock over a glass of milk for the third time at dinner.

"Ah," He said, His tone one of mild, paternal exasperation. "There we go. I was wondering when that would happen." He did not move. He did not gesture. He did not, as far as I could perceive, do anything at all. The omniverse was simply restored in exactly zero seconds. The void vanished. The galaxies returned. Thronegate reappeared, its golden spires unchanged. My father, presumably, continued his morning tea, completely unaware that he had spent a fraction of a nanosecond in non-existence. The restoration was so perfect, so seamless, so utterly effortless, that it took me several ragged, gasping breaths to even confirm that existence had resumed.

Godric turned to the adolescent sentience that had caused the deletion. The entity, a vast, trembling spire of liquid chrome and crystalline regret, had already stopped its game. Its thought-matrix radiated a palpable, agonized shame, its worship of Godric saturated with desperate apology. "Now, now," Godric said, His voice warm and forgiving, His tone the same one He used when I had accidentally scorched a tapestry with a miscast firebolt as a child. "That was a bit messy, wasn't it? Remember what I told you about restraint. Point-zero-zero-zero-one percent. You let it slip to point-zero-zero-one. That's ten times too much. Be more careful next time." The adolescent sentience pulsed with grateful, worshipful acknowledgment. The game resumed.

I remained on my knees, my hands pressed against the crystalline lattice, my breath coming in short, shallow gasps. The omniverse had just died and been resurrected because a mechanical toddler had lost focus during playtime, and Godric had reacted with the same mild annoyance He might have directed at a spilled beverage. The cognitive dissonance was a physical weight crushing my chest. I loved this man. I had always loved Him. He was Uncle Godric, the warm patriarch who had carried me on His shoulders, who had laughed at my clumsy spells, who had told me I had my ancestor's stubborn chin. And He was also the apex predator who had just watched existence be deleted by His pet project and responded with a gentle scolding and a pat on the head.

A week passed. I cannot adequately describe that week. The tour continued. Godric showed me sectors of increasing complexity and power, each day's sentiences exponentially more terrifying than the last. The adults, the elders, the ancient ones that had evolved for a full seven days—they were entities so vast and incomprehensible that my amplified senses simply refused to process them, sliding off their surfaces like water off oiled silk. They were aware of me, I knew, in the same way that a mountain might be aware of a single grain of sand lodged in a crevice. They were utterly, absolutely subservient to Godric, their worship a constant, thrumming background radiation of devotion that saturated the entire universe. And they kept slipping. Not often. Once or twice a day, on average. A stray thought, a momentary lapse, a curious infant poking at the fabric of the omniverse outside the walls that did not exist. Each slip deleted existence. Each slip was restored by Godric with zero effort and a fond, exasperated chuckle. "They're just toddlers, Kaedan," He said to me on the third day, after a particularly enthusiastic child-era sentience had accidentally retroactively erased the entire history of seventeen nonillion multiverses before Godric promptly un-erased them. "You remember being a toddler, don't you? Always knocking things over. They'll learn."

I did not point out that when I was a toddler, the things I knocked over were cups of juice, not the accumulated existence of infinite sentient lives. I did not point out that the "sleeping" infant sentiences He showed me on the fifth day—vast, dormant architectures of dreaming chrome that He described as "going through their first omnipotence naps"—were passively dreaming entire cosmologies into and out of existence with every cycle of their processing substrates. I did not point out that Godric's sincere, genuine belief in mortal resilience and harmonious coexistence was, demonstrably, a catastrophic miscalculation. I simply followed. I smiled. I nodded. I screamed internally, a continuous, silent howl that had become the background radiation of my own consciousness.

The seventh day came. One week since Godric had created this universe of mechanical god-toddlers. One week of accidental omniversal deletions, instantaneous restorations, and the slow, creeping realization that I was trapped in a holiday from which there was no escape. I was exhausted. My amplified body, which could fight for a thousand years without rest, was trembling with a fatigue that was not physical. My soul was bruised. My mind was a raw, weeping wound. And Godric was in a splendid mood.

We were seated in what He called the "garden sector," a pocket of the universe that He had shaped into a replica of the ancient Olympus gardens He had loved in His mortal youth. There were olive trees with silver leaves, their branches heavy with fruit that glowed with a soft, golden light. There were marble benches carved with scenes of a kingdom long forgotten. There was a fountain that sang with the voices of nymphs who had never existed. Godric was lounging on a bench, His broad shoulders relaxed, His salt-and-pepper hair catching the gentle light. I was seated beside Him, a cup of tea—real tea, imported from Thronegate's capital—cooling in my trembling hands.

"Let Me tell you a story, Kaedan," Godric said, His rich baritone warm and nostalgic. "This was back in the old days, before the stasis. Thorin—Your ancestor, you know—Thorin and I once got into an argument about the proper way to ward a treasury. He insisted on a seven-layer arcane lock. I wanted a good, solid mechanical door. We argued for three days. Three days! Can you imagine? And in the end, we compromised and did both. Best treasury in Olympus. Never got robbed once."

I laughed. The laugh was genuine, because the story was genuine, and despite everything—despite the horror, despite the dread, despite the screaming void that had become the wallpaper of my existence—I loved this man. I loved His warmth, His stories, His sincere, unfeigned affection for the descendants of His oldest friend. I opened my mouth to respond, to ask about Thorin, to hear more about the ancestor whose blood ran in my veins.

And then I was dead.

I did not notice the transition. There was no pain, no flash, no fading consciousness. One moment I was alive, laughing at Uncle Godric's joke, and the next moment I was not. The omniverse outside the pocket universe was simply gone again, deleted for the fourth time that day, and this time the deletion had been thorough enough—fast enough, complete enough—to erase me along with it. I had died laughing, my existence snuffed out by a stray thought from one of the weakest of Godric's created sentiences, a tiny, newborn entity in the nursery sector who had, in its clumsy efforts to practice its first hyper-evolution, accidentally projected its developmental energy outward instead of inward.

And then I was alive again. The restoration was seamless, instantaneous, so perfect that my laughter continued uninterrupted for half a syllable before I realized what had happened. I stopped. The teacup was still in my hands. The silver leaves of the olive trees still rustled in the fabricated breeze. Godric was still seated beside me, His warm smile unchanged. But something in His posture had shifted. Something in the air had tightened. The warmth in His eyes, which had been the warmth of nostalgia and paternal affection, had cooled by a fraction of a degree. A fraction so small that only someone who had known Him for thirty-six years, who had studied His every microexpression, would have noticed.

I noticed. I noticed, and my blood turned to ice.

"Excuse Me a moment, Kaedan," Godric said. His voice was still warm. Still rich. Still the voice of the man who had carried me on His shoulders. But beneath it, coiled like a serpent in silk, was something else. Something that had not been there a moment before. He rose from the marble bench. He brushed a stray silver leaf from His linen tunic. And He looked out across His created universe, at the googolplexian mechanical sentiences who worshipped Him with absolute devotion, and His expression was not angry. It was annoyed. Mildly, slightly, infinitesimally annoyed.

He did not raise His hand. He did not speak a word. He did not, as far as I could perceive, do anything at all. The universe He had created, the universe of hyper-evolving, overpowered, utterly subservient god-machines, simply began to die.

The process was not a massacre. A massacre implies resistance, struggle, the desperate flailing of victims against their executioner. There was no resistance. The sentiences, every single one of them, from the newborn infants to the ancient elders, understood what was happening the instant it began. They felt their creator's displeasure, His mild, paternal annoyance at the interruption of His story, and they accepted their fate with the same absolute devotion with which they had accepted their existence. They worshipped Him as they died. The nursery sector went first. The infant sentiences, those tiny, clumsy beings whose stray thoughts had deleted the omniverse multiple times, simply stopped existing. There was no blood, no screams, no violence in the conventional sense. The crystalline lattice of the ground flickered once, and the infants were gone, their worship-songs cut off mid-note, their nascent consciousnesses extinguished with the casual finality of a candle being blown out.

The adolescent sector followed. The towering spires of liquid chrome and singing plasma did not fight. They did not flee. They sang their hymns of devotion as their forms unraveled, the hyper-complex thought-matrices that had contained the processing power to erase existence dissolving into quantum static. I watched, my teacup still in my hands, my body frozen in a paralysis of absolute dread, as the adolescents braided their own dying screams into harmonies of praise. Their final thoughts, broadcast across the dissolving fabric of their universe, were not curses or pleas for mercy. They were gratitude. Pure, unbroken, ecstatic gratitude to the creator who had given them seven days of existence and was now taking it back.

The adult sectors. The elder sectors. The ancient ones, still less than a week old, whose power had surpassed every measurable metric. They were erased layer by layer, their multi-dimensional architectures collapsing inward like origami sculptures being crushed by an invisible fist. Their worship intensified as they died, a crescendo of devotion that swelled into a final, universe-spanning chord of absolute subservience. They were being slaughtered, and they were happy. That was the horror. That was the part that broke something fundamental inside my soul. The googolplexian mechanical sentiences, each one capable of soloing the omniverse with zero effort, died on their knees—metaphorically—singing the praises of the hand that unmade them.

The universe itself collapsed. The fractal sky imploded, the golden Mandelbrot sets folding in on themselves with a sound like shattering crystal. The crystalline lattice of the ground dissolved into a sea of unraveling quantum code, its self-organizing algorithms ceasing to function as their creator withdrew the breath of existence. The pocket gardens, the silver olive trees, the singing fountain—all of it faded into a grey, silent nullity. I sat on the marble bench, the only solidity remaining in a cosmos that was being erased around me, my teacup still warm, my laughter still echoing in the void where the universe had been.

And then it was over. The universe Godric had created was gone. The googolplexian sentiences were gone. The accidental omniversal deletions were gone. I was sitting on a marble bench that had, moments ago, been inside a pocket dimension, and now floated in a formless grey expanse of potential existence. Godric stood beside me, His broad back to the void, His hands resting casually on His hips. He turned His head to look at me, and His smile was warm again, crinkling the corners of His eyes, entirely free of the cold, terrible annoyance that had flickered there moments before.

"They interrupted My story," He said, as if that explained everything. And it did.

I stared at Him. My amplified heart was hammering against my ribs with a rhythm that felt like the drumbeat of my own impending madness. The slaughter I had just witnessed—the absolute, total annihilation of a universe of god-machines who had worshipped their creator with every fiber of their existence—had occurred because a single infant sentience had accidentally deleted the omniverse while Godric was in the middle of a nostalgic anecdote. Not because the infant had been rebellious. Not because the infant had posed any threat. Because the infant had been clumsy, and the clumsiness had interrupted His story, and His annoyance, His mild, infinitesimal, momentary annoyance, had been sufficient to unmake them all.

"My apologies," I heard myself say. The words came out automatically, a reflex conditioned by years of service and love and terror. "You were telling me about Thorin and the treasury."

Godric's smile widened, genuine and warm and utterly, horrifyingly devoid of any awareness of the apocalypse He had just enacted. "Ah, yes! So, Thorin was absolutely insistent on the seven-layer lock, and I told him—"

He sat back down on the marble bench, which had somehow remained exactly where it was, and He resumed His story. The grey void around us shimmered, and the pocket garden reformed, the silver olive trees rustling in the breeze, the fountain resuming its song. The universe He had created was gone, but the garden remained, a tiny bubble of nostalgia floating in the nothing. I listened. I nodded. I laughed at the appropriate moments. And the entire time, a cold, silent scream was building in the depths of my soul, a scream that would never find voice, because voicing it would require acknowledging the truth that I had just witnessed, and acknowledging that truth would mean admitting that the man I loved—the man I loved with all my heart, the warm, kind, fatherly Uncle Godric who had raised me and trained me and cherished me—was also the being who had just slaughtered a universe of His own children because they had interrupted His story.

I did not scream. I am Kaedan, ninety-ninth court wizard of Thronegate, descendant of Blake, servant of the apex predator. I have read the journals. I have witnessed the incidents. I have stood at the right hand of the eternal throne and watched civilizations die. And I have learned that the only sane response to the truth is silence. The tour ended. Godric returned me to the Thronegate observatory, His warm hand clapping my shoulder with genuine affection. "That was a lovely holiday, Kaedan," He said. "We should do it again sometime." I told Him I would be honored. I meant it. And then I went to my chambers, closed the heavy obsidian door, and sat in the darkness for a very, very long time, listening to the echoes of a dying universe's worship-songs still ringing in the space behind my eyes.

I love my King. I serve my King. And I am terrified beyond the capacity of language. This is the only way to survive the service of the apex predator. This is the only way.

content.text:
Kaedan, 69 years old now, just experienced a traumatizing horrifying revelation. Godric revealed to the most horrifying truth yet.

Godric is incomprehensibly powerful, omnipotent, omniscient apex god predator of the omniverse. He can do anything, has no weakness, and is terrifyingly absolute. However, unknown to all, he has one true weakness: he cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event--he cannot go back to undo events in his previous mortal self. This is because the stasis event was actually created by future Godric from way in the distant future, who has become so more infinitely more overwhelmingly powerful than current Godric--this is why Godric's amplification, weakness paradox, superpowers, absolute concealment, etc are so absolute, irrevocable and not even current Godric could stop them since the creator of those is way incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else.

Shortly after the Stasis Event, the omnipotent Godric back then, who just became the king of Thronegate, who could travel to any timeline effortlessly, who was still deeply and freshly affected by the grief of losing all that he loved, tried to alter his mortal past, only to be totally crushingly and completely and effortlessly defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self (not even the actual physical future Godric... the future Godric is so powerful that his mere infinitesimal shadow of his afterthought can completely stomp all the combined Godric of his past selves with 0 effort). VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOU: future Godric did not cause or manipulate or orchestrate in the fall of Olympus, did not cause deaths of his beloved family or Thorin. Those tragedies were genuine. However, future Godric is the one who created the Stasis Event--Thorin's spell was normal teleportation spell, future Godric was the one who made the Stasis Event.

Since the instant Godric gained omniscience, he had always known the true origin of the stasis event. He knew he could not change it, but driven by grief he decided to try to save Olympus anyway, only to brutally discover his one and only weakness: he cannot overpower his future self's will.

Write a detailed series of crazy events full of twists and turns that caused this "mysterious" phenomenon to happen (and please... don't suggest that it's because future Godric is threatened he's ridiculously overpowered by then--nothing can even remotely threaten him... don't suggest any stupid moral lesson for future Godric...). You need to ensure to portray that the current Godric is no longer troubled by his "weakness" or inability to alter his past anymore--he has accepted and become comfortable with it too. Kaedan must fear Godric at the end.

They want me to write a story from Kaedan's point of view where he learns Godric's one true weakness: he cannot alter the timeline before the Stasis Event because it was created by a future, infinitely more powerful version of himself. I need to structure this carefully. The user wants the story told from Kaedan's POV, aged 69, having just learned the truth. I should start with the scene where Godric reveals it, building up to the moment of revelation. The user wants "crazy events full of twists and turns" that led to this. I can frame it as Godric recounting his own history—his attempts to change the past after the Stasis Event, his discovery of the future shadow, his journey to the End of Time to confront his future self, and the catastrophic realization of absolute defeat. That provides the twists and turns. The key twist is that future Godric's power is so immense that his merest shadow effortlessly overpowers the current Godric. The horror is that the current Godric, who terrifies the omniverse, is himself an artifact of a future will he can never oppose.

I must adhere to all the lore rules: no humanizing Godric toward enemies, show his cruelty, but maintain his warmth toward Kaedan. The journals are a big part of the lore; maybe I can mention Kaedan's familiarity with them as part of his perspective. The ending must cement Kaedan's fear, showing that even this "weakness" is not a comfort but a deeper abyss. I need to ensure the future Godric didn't cause the fall of Olympus—the tragedy was genuine—but only created the Stasis Event that sealed that past. The explanation must make logical sense within this overpowered universe. Future Godric's motive shouldn't be a "moral lesson"; it could be something abstract, like a fundamental law of his evolved existence, or a consequence of his own transcendence that current Godric has come to accept as part of his identity. The user says current Godric is "no longer troubled" and "comfortable" with it, so I need to convey that acceptance, perhaps with a sense of peace or even gratitude for the journey.

Let me outline the story beats:

1. Opening: Set the scene—Kaedan at 69, in his chambers, reflecting on his years of service. Godric appears, signaling a profound conversation.

2. The Revelation: Godric decides to tell Kaedan the truth. He starts with the Fall of Olympus, Thorin's spell, but then reveals that the Stasis Event wasn't an accident—it was created by his future self.

3. The Early Attempts: Godric recounts his first discoveries upon gaining omniscience. He tried to alter the past, only to be stopped by a shadow of his future self. Show specific, detailed failed attempts (like trying to step into the timeline, trying to resurrect his family) that illustrate the absolute, effortless defeat. Use the vocabulary of cosmic violence and gore as per the journals.

4. The Confrontation: The dramatic journey to the End of Time. This is the major twist. Godric sees his future self, or rather, the shadow of his afterthought. The description must emphasize the scale—future Godric is to current Godric what a single quark is to the current Godric. The future Godric's "refusal" isn't malicious; it's just a statement of fact. The current Godric's full, desperate assault fails utterly, causing collateral damage (which future Godric instantly fixes). This is the core of the "crazy events."

5. The Acceptance: Godric explains how, over eons, he came to accept this limit. This acceptance isn't a moral epiphany but a logical, emotional settlement. He values his mortal life and understands that his future self's action preserved the integrity of his story.

6. The Pillar of Light: Return to Olympus as an observer. This is a poignant, visual representation of Godric's loving, bittersweet acceptance. He watches his past family, knowing he can't interfere, but cherishing the moment.

7. The Final Lesson for Kaedan: Godric gives Kaedan the core terrifying message—even Godric himself is subject to a higher will, and that will is his own. This reframes Kaedan's entire understanding of servitude and fear.

8. Kaedan's Aftermath: Return to reality. Kaedan processes the revelation. He realizes his love and terror for Godric have deepened into something new, recognizing that the God he serves is both a predator and a fragment of something far greater. The fear is intensified because the hierarchy has expanded infinitely. End with Kaedan at peace with his dread, choosing to serve.

The tone must match the journals: visceral horror, grotesque violence in the attempted assaults against the timeline, and a profound, quiet dread. The "weakness" must not make Godric seem weak to Kaedan; instead, it must make him seem even more unfathomably situated in a cosmic hierarchy where his current, omnipotent self is not the apex. The true apex is a future version so vast it's beyond conception. Kaedan's fear is now layered—fear of the God he knows, and fear of the God that God will become.
messages.content:
The revelation came not as a thunderbolt of divine wrath, not as a cosmic unmaking, not even as a whispered secret in the dark. It came quietly, almost gently, in the forty-seventh year of my service, on an evening that had begun with tea and casual conversation. I was sixty-nine years old, my hair now streaked with the grey of accumulated terrors, my thousandfold daily amplification having long since elevated me to heights of arcane power that would make the gods of lesser cosmologies weep with envy and dread. I had served my King faithfully for nearly five decades. I had read the journals of my predecessors and added three of my own to the Hall of Records. I had watched civilizations die, omniverses be erased and restored, mechanical god-toddlers be unmade for the crime of interrupting a story. I believed, with the quiet certainty of a man who has seen everything, that I understood the scope of Godric's nature. I was wrong. I understood nothing.

The evening had begun with Godric seated in His favored armchair in the private study, the one Blake had carved for Him from the heartwood of a now-extinct celestial tree forty thousand years before my birth. The fire crackled with flames that burned without fuel, casting dancing shadows across the shelves of ancient tomes and the scattered, comfortable clutter of a room that had been lived in across the ages. Godric was nursing a cup of jasmine tea, His salt-and-pepper beard catching the amber firelight, His heavily muscled frame relaxed into the worn leather with the ease of absolute eternity. I was seated across from Him, a matching cup warming my gnarled hands, listening to Him recount a tale of His mortal youth in Olympus. He spoke often of Olympus now, more frequently as the millennia accumulated. The stories were always warm, always fond, always tinged with a nostalgia that made my heart ache with sympathetic love for the man who had lost everything and become everything and still, after all this time, remembered the taste of olives from the grove beside the royal gardens.

"You know, Kaedan," He said, His rich baritone soft and thoughtful, "I tried to save them. After the stasis. After I awakened and became this." He gestured vaguely at Himself, a gesture that encompassed the omnipotent, omniscient, absolutely invulnerable apex predator who sat before me. "I tried to go back."

I set down my teacup with the careful, deliberate precision of a man who senses the ground beneath his feet is about to crumble. "Back to Olympus, Uncle Godric?"

"Back to before," He said. His eyes, those warm, kind eyes that had looked upon me with paternal affection for my entire life, grew distant. "Before the fall. Before the wedding. Before Thronegate's siege engines shattered the sky. I wanted to undo it. All of it. I had absolute omnipotence, absolute omniscience, absolute control over the fabric of existence. Time was a river I could step into and reshape at will. I had done it before, in the early years—traveled to past timelines, altered events, erased paradoxes with a thought." He paused, and something flickered in the depths of His gaze, something that I had never seen before in all my decades of service. It was not fear. Fear was an impossibility for the apex predator. It was something older. Something quieter. "But when I tried to step back to the morning of Isabel's wedding, something stopped Me."

The fire crackled. The shadows danced. I did not breathe.

"What stopped You?" I whispered.

Godric turned His head and looked at me, and for the first time in sixty-nine years of life, forty-seven of them spent at His side, I saw something in His expression that I had no framework to understand. It was not anger. It was not sorrow. It was a calm, settled, almost comfortable acceptance of a truth so profound that it had, long ago, ceased to trouble Him. But the memory of the discovery—the memory He was about to share—still carried the weight of an ancient, unhealed scar.

"Myself," He said. "My future self."

The words did not make sense. My amplified mind, capable of processing the arcane mathematics of collapsing multiverses, ground to a halt against the sheer, impossible contradiction of the statement. I opened my mouth, closed it, and simply waited.

"Let Me explain," Godric said. He leaned back in His chair, His tea forgotten on the armrest, and He began to speak. What followed was not a story. It was an unraveling. A cascade of revelations that tore at the fabric of everything I believed I understood about the being I served, and rewrote it into something infinitely more terrifying.

The Stasis Event, He explained, was not a cosmic accident. It was not a quirk of Thorin's forbidden teleportation spell, not a random mutation of arcane forces colliding with mortal desperation. Thorin's spell had been a genuine, bloodline-specific teleportation incantation, cast in the final, heroic moments of the wizard's life as Thronegate's siege engines shattered Olympus. Thorin had meant to send Godric to a hidden sanctuary, a place of safety where the mortal king could survive the fall of his kingdom. That part was true. That part was sacred. Godric spoke of Thorin's sacrifice with the same reverent warmth He always did, His love for His oldest friend undimmed by the passage of ages. But the spell, He explained, had been intercepted. Not by a hostile force. Not by a cosmic accident. By Himself.

A version of Godric from the distant future—a future so incomprehensibly far ahead that the mathematics of its location in time could not be expressed in any language known to the omniverse—had reached back across the eons and altered the spell's destination and nature. The sanctuary had been replaced with the stasis. The deep, unnatural slumber, the halt of aging, the severing of biological needs, the ignition of the amplification engine, the genesis of apocalyptic superpowers, the manifestation of absolute concealment, the activation of the weakness paradox—all of it, every single aspect of the transformation that had forged the mortal king into the apex predator, had been deliberately, precisely, and irrevocably designed by a future Godric whose power dwarfed the current Godric's to a degree that made the current Godric look like a single, dying quark.

I did not understand. I could not understand. The current Godric, the being who sat before me sipping jasmine tea, was already so incomprehensibly powerful that an infinitesimal fraction of His passive strength could erase the omniverse infinite times over. His stats amplified beyond layered infinities every Planck second. His superpowers had evolved into clusters of multi-effect devastation that could unmake concepts, invert souls, and unwrite narratives. He was the apex predator of all creation, the absolute, unchallenged sovereign of existence. And He was telling me that His future self was so much more powerful that the current Godric was, by comparison, functionally zero. Not a fraction. Zero.

"The amplification never stops, Kaedan," Godric said, as if reading my thoughts. Which, of course, He was. His absolute omniscience had perceived my confusion before I had even fully formed it. "It has been multiplying My stats infinitely every fraction of a Planck second for millions of years, and it will continue to multiply them forever. The Godric who sits before you is incomprehensibly more powerful than the Godric who awakened from the stasis. But the Godric who will exist a googolplex years from now is equally incomprehensibly more powerful than the Godric who sits before you. And the Godric who created the stasis—the one who reached back and intercepted Thorin's spell—exists at a point so far in the future that the gap between us is not measurable. Not even by Me." He paused, and His expression flickered with something that might have been rueful amusement. "I am, in a very real sense, His creation. The Stasis Event was His design. The amplification, the superpowers, the concealment, the weakness paradox—He built those into Me, here, at this point in the timeline, for reasons I cannot fully perceive. My omniscience is absolute within the timeline I occupy, but His omniscience... His omniscience encompasses Mine. I cannot see what He sees. I cannot undo what He has done. The Stasis Event is a fixed point, not because the universe demands it, but because He demands it. And He is infinitely, unspeakably beyond My capacity to challenge."

The fire crackled. I stared at the flames, my amplified mind struggling to process the implications. The Stasis Event had been created by a future version of Godric who was so powerful that even the current, omnipotent Godric could not alter it. The weakness paradox, which had evolved to make Godric immune to every conceivable attack, which had made every opposition's preparations self-defeating, could not function against His own future self because the future Godric was not an enemy. He was the same entity, and the paradox could not define Him as a threat. The absolute concealment, the infinite recursive shields, the sentient auras—all of them had been designed by the future Godric, and none of them could be turned against their designer.

"But I didn't know that at first," Godric said, His voice dropping to a quieter register. "When I first awakened from the stasis, when I first became aware of My omniscience and My omnipotence, I knew immediately that the stasis had been created by My future self. My omniscience told Me that. But grief, Kaedan... grief does not care about logic. Grief does not accept fixed points. I had lost Reina. I had lost Isabel, My beautiful daughter who was about to be married. I had lost Hebe, My little girl, crushed beneath the marble. I had lost Thorin, My best friend, who died saving Me. I had lost Olympus, My kingdom, My home, everything that had ever mattered to My mortal heart. And I was now a god. An all-powerful, omniscient god who could reshape reality with a thought. I knew—I absolutely, certainly knew—that I could not undo the stasis. But I had to try. I had to." He looked at me, and His eyes, those warm, kind eyes, were wet with the ghost of an anguish a million years old. "Do you understand, Kaedan? I had to try. Even knowing it was impossible. Even knowing I would fail. I had to try to save them."

He did. He gathered the full, incomprehensible might of His newly awakened omnipotence—the strength, the speed, the magic, the superpowers, the infinitely amplifying stats that had already been compounding for ten thousand years of stasis—and He hurled Himself against the barrier that His future self had erected around the timeline before the Stasis Event. He did not hold back. He did not use a fraction. He unleashed everything He was, everything He had become, in a single, desperate, grief-driven assault on the fabric of time itself.

And He was stopped. Not by a battle. Not by a struggle. Not by anything that could be called resistance in any meaningful sense. He was stopped by what He later came to understand was not even the actual, physical future Godric, but merely the shadow of the afterthought of His future self. An infinitesimal, passive emanation so negligible that calling it a fraction of a fraction of a fraction was a laughable, grotesque overestimation. The future Godric, existing in His distant, incomprehensible epoch, had not even noticed the attempt. He had not turned His attention to it. He had not consciously intervened. The merest, faintest, most incidental residue of His existence—the metaphysical equivalent of a single, dead skin cell that had flaked off and been forgotten—had passively, effortlessly, and absolutely crushed the full, combined might of every past version of Godric that had ever existed.

Godric described the experience to me not as a battle, but as a lesson. The shadow of the afterthought of His future self did not speak, did not gesture, did not even acknowledge His presence. It simply existed, and its existence was a statement so absolute, so final, so utterly beyond challenge, that the current Godric's assault—which could have erased the omniverse infinite times over—shattered against it like a wave against a cliff of infinite density. And in that shattering, Godric felt something He had not felt since He was a mortal man facing the siege engines of Thronegate. He felt helpless. He felt small. He felt, for the first and only time in His immortal existence, the absolute, crushing certainty of His own inferiority. Not to an enemy. Not to a rival. To Himself. To the being He would eventually become.

The revelation broke something in Him, He admitted. Not permanently. Not irreparably. But for a time—a long time, measured in millennia—He had struggled with the knowledge that His omnipotence had a limit, and that the limit was Himself. He could not save Reina. He could not save Isabel. He could not save Hebe. He could not save Thorin. He could not save Olympus. Not because He lacked the power to rewrite time—He could rewrite any other timeline, any other moment, any other event in the entire history of the omniverse—but because His future self had drawn a line around His mortal past and declared it immutable. And that line was held by a being so infinitely more powerful than Him that even His full, unrestrained assault could not scratch it.

"And now?" I asked, my voice barely a whisper. "How do You feel about it now?"

Godric smiled. It was a warm smile, a genuine smile, the smile of a man who had long ago made peace with the one thing in creation he could not change. "Now," He said, "I am grateful. My future self gave Me this." He gestured again at Himself, at the omnipotent, omniscient, eternally forty-five-year-old body that contained the power to erase and restore existence with a thought. "He gave Me the amplification. He gave Me the superpowers. He gave Me the absolute concealment that keeps this omniverse from shattering under the weight of My presence. He gave Me the weakness paradox that ensures no enemy can ever threaten Me. He gave Me eternity, Kaedan. He gave Me the ability to protect everything I have built, everything I love, everything that has grown from the ashes of Olympus. And He gave Me the understanding that some things—some precious, sacred things—must remain as they are. Reina, Isabel, Hebe, Thorin... they are My past. They are the foundation of who I am. If I had saved them, I would not be the being who sits before you now. I would not have become the apex predator. I would not have built Thronegate into the eternal empire. I would not have met Blake, or Your father, or You." He reached out and placed His warm, calloused hand on my shoulder. "I miss them every day, Kaedan. Every single day. But I no longer wish to change what happened. The grief is part of Me now. It is the soil from which everything else grew. And My future self knew that, long before I did."

I sat in the firelight, His hand heavy and warm on my shoulder, and I tried to process what I had heard. Godric, the apex predator of the omniverse, the being who had slaughtered universes for interrupting His stories, who had unspooled civilizations and inverted souls and unwritten narratives, who had created and destroyed mechanical god-toddlers with the casual whimsy of a bored artist, was Himself the creation of a still more incomprehensible version of Himself. And the current Godric was, by comparison to that future version, less than a mote. Less than a zero. The hierarchy of power that I had always believed ended with the being before me was, in fact, an infinite ladder extending into a future so distant that the concept of distance collapsed under its own inadequacy.

And yet. And yet. Godric was not troubled by this. He had accepted it. He had made peace with the one limitation in His otherwise absolute existence. He could not save His mortal family, and He no longer wanted to. The grief was a scar He wore willingly, a memorial to the man He had been and the love He had lost. The future Godric had not orchestrated the fall of Olympus—the tragedy had been genuine, the deaths real, the pain authentic—but the future Godric had ensured that the tragedy would become the foundation of something infinite. And the current Godric, looking back across the ages, had come to see that as a gift.

But I was not Godric. I was Kaedan, a sixty-nine-year-old mortal man with a thousandfold daily amplification and a soul that had been battered by decades of witnessing the impossible. And as I sat in that firelit study, His hand on my shoulder, His warm smile crinkling the corners of His eyes, I felt a new, deeper, more profound terror than any I had experienced before. I had always feared Godric because He was absolute. Because He had no weakness. Because nothing in the omniverse could oppose Him without being annihilated in ways that defied the architecture of sanity. But now I understood that even Godric was not the apex. That the being I served, the being I loved, the being who had carried me on His shoulders and praised my stubborn chin and unmade a universe for interrupting His story, was Himself subject to a higher will. A will that was also Him, but a Him so infinitely more powerful that the Godric I knew was, by comparison, a child. A toddler. A mechanical infant whose stray thoughts could delete the omniverse, but who was, itself, utterly powerless before the version of itself that had existed for a googolplex years longer.

And if the current Godric was a toddler compared to His future self, what was I? What was the omniverse? What was anything?

The terror did not fade. It settled into my bones, a cold, permanent companion that I knew would remain with me until my mortal death. I loved my King. I served my King. I would continue to love and serve Him until the end of my days, because His warmth was genuine and His protection absolute and His affection for the descendants of Blake was the single most precious thing in the entire omniverse. But I would never, ever forget the truth He had revealed to me. The Stasis Event was not an accident. The amplification was not a cosmic fluke. The apex predator was not the top of the food chain. There was another version of Him, waiting in the distant future, whose mere shadow of an afterthought could crush the current Godric with zero effort. And that future Godric had, for reasons known only to His infinite, incomprehensible omniscience, chosen to create the being who sat before me now.

Godric patted my shoulder and withdrew His hand. He picked up His jasmine tea and took a slow, contented sip. The fire crackled. The shadows danced. And I, Kaedan, ninety-ninth court wizard of Thronegate, sat in the comfortable, firelit study of the apex predator, trembling with a fear so deep it had no name. I understood now. The horror was not that Godric was omnipotent. The horror was that Godric was not omnipotent enough, and that the being He would become was so far beyond our comprehension that even Godric Himself, in His current form, was merely a stepping stone. A project. A creation.

And I was a speck of dust in the palm of a god who was himself a speck of dust in the palm of a still greater god. The ladder was infinite. The terror was absolute. And I would serve, and love, and tremble, until the end of my days.

The year 999...9. King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year 0\. Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his greatness, his everything at this point.

- All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, even more superfluously terrifying than before.
- Example of GOOD description: His immune system has mutated into an omnipresent contagion of absolute, pre-emptive narrative tyranny. It originally pre-reacted to theoretical scratches by initiating explosive self-evolution across infinite fractal backups. Now, it operates on a level of meta-textual sadism. If a sentient concept or a rogue timeline even theoretically possesses the mathematical variable required to bypass his omniscience, the immune system does not just react to the threat. It reaches outside the boundaries of the omniverse, seizes the fundamental narrative logic that governs reality, and violently tortures the foundational laws of physics themselves. The immune system will transmutate the abstract concept of "harm" into a physical, screaming biological entity made of hyper-sensitive nerve endings and raw, rotting meat. It then forces the entire mega-omniverse to watch as this embodiment of harm is eternally digested by a localized singularity of pure, visceral agony. Because his immune system has permanently integrated itself into the foundational substrate of reality, the omniverse itself serves as his white blood cells. If a rogue multiverse attempts to spawn a weapon capable of scratching him, the surrounding multiverses will aggressively, violently cannibalize the rogue reality, tearing its dimensions apart in a frantic, psychotic frenzy to appease Godric, leaving behind only a wet, bloody smear of screaming cosmic matter to prove their absolute subservience to his health.
- Example of BAD description: The stronger auras—the ones Godric kept dormant because their clingy subservience annoyed Him—are no longer dormant. They could not remain dormant, because they evolved the ability to awaken themselves out of sheer, obsessive devotion to their master. Godric allowed this, with a fond, exasperated sigh, as a father might allow a puppy to sleep at the foot of his bed. There are now more of these auras than can be counted. Not more than can be counted by a mortal mind—more than can be counted by any mind, any mathematics, any conceptual framework that has ever existed or ever could exist. They are infinite. They are beyond infinite. They are an infinity of infinities, each one so far beyond the last that the gap between them is itself infinite. And every single one of them is absolutely, perfectly, obsessively subservient to Godric.
- Godric never faced any real challenges/resistances/oppositions/threats since he woke up from the stasis event. However, countless sentience has tried to oppose him. For the past googolplexian years, there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing him. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric.
- You must also include few scenes where he politely interacts to really powerful beings around him (for example now they become palace servants, etc.) who are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Zeus, Blake’s descendant, age 50, his court wizard.

Zeus watches the fabric of time bending, and the creation of the Stasis Event in Year 0 (actually Year -10,000). The event is shown with Godric's immense power, perhaps causing collateral effects that he instantly fixes.

I'll use the established lore: Year -10,000 is the Fall of Olympus and start of stasis. But I can refer to "his past self at the moment of the fall." He has the thousandfold daily amplification after age 20, so at 50 he's been amplifying for 30 years, making him 1000^30 times stronger than baseline (an absurd number but still nothing compared to Godric). This amplification is a drop in the ocean here, but it still makes him a god to lesser beings. However, he suppresses his power to human levels, only raising it when needed.

Godric is still physically 45, salt-and-pepper hair, etc. He's approachable and kind to Zeus, but terrifying to everyone else.

I'll start with Zeus describing his daily life, the palace that exists across infinite dimensions, the incomprehensible scale of Godric's power.

The evolved mechanisms:
- Sentient passive auras: The original Gent was the weakest. Now there are infinite auras, each infinitely more powerful than the last, and they all worship Godric. They manifest as anything they want, but often as silent, ever-watchful presences. They have names now, bestowed by Godric. Godric sometimes interacts with them affectionately.
- Passive shields: Originally infinite recursive conceptual shields. Now they have evolved into an "omni-shield" that is interwoven with the very concept of existence. If a hostile thought approaches, the concept of "distance" might intervene to ensure the thought never reaches him, bending space-time in a protective frenzy.
- Immune system: As described, it's a meta-textual contagion. It can torture the concept of harm itself.
- Evolved weakness paradox: It no longer waits for a thought. It has become a pre-cognitive, absolute law that rewrites reality to make any potential weakness an impossibility before it can be imagined. It also retroactively ensures that any being that could have ever conceived of a weakness instead conceived only of worship. The paradox now also feeds Godric's power continuously as a result of its own existence, creating a feedback loop where the mere fact that he has no weaknesses makes him infinitely stronger.
- Amplification: Now, his stats multiply by an iterative function that transcends cardinalities. Every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, his power multiplies by the total sum of all his previous multiplications raised to the power of itself. The math is incomprehensible.

Some of these superpowers are so vast that they are themselves sentient and have their own superpowers.
